data.title: OpenAI Wants to Go Public. First Sarah Friar Needs to Get It to Grow Up. — WSJ
data.description:
By Berber Jin and Corrie Driebusch

After a dizzying summer of deals, Sam Altman came to work one day last fall, ready to show just how confident he was in OpenAI's future. In a livestream broadcast to the public from the startup's unmarked San Francisco offices in October, he showed a black-and-white slide with a number he said represented OpenAI's financial obligations for new computing capacity: $1.4 trillion.

The giant number spread like wildfire across the investment world — and not in a good way. OpenAI was set to generate $13 billion in revenue that year. So everyone was asking the same question: how on earth was the company going to pay for it?

For Sarah Friar, OpenAI's chief financial officer, it was time to clear up the confusion. A veteran CFO who had spent more than a decade at Goldman Sachs, she had been brought into the startup to give it the financial maturity needed to go public.

In the ensuing months, she privately walked back his comments. The company actually planned to spend a much smaller amount, she told investors: $600 billion through 2030. More recently, she has questioned the wisdom of spending even more money on data centers, at times putting her at odds with her freewheeling boss.

Like any corporate CFO, Friar must translate her CEO's grandest ambitions into something the company can actually afford — and know the right time to say no. She follows a line of women in Silicon Valley asked to tame the wilder impulses of companies — or their younger male founders. Google brought in Ruth Porat after going public to control costs and add discipline to what had become freewheeling spending on moonshots. Mark Zuckerberg got Sheryl Sandberg at Facebook, and Elon Musk has Gwynne Shotwell at SpaceX.

There was Friar herself at Square in 2012, where she became Jack Dorsey's second-in-command, helping steer the payments startup through a turbulent public offering more than a decade ago. When she announced her departure in 2018, Square's stock sank by over 10% — a sign of just how important investors came to see her role at the company.

It falls to Friar to pull off what is envisioned as one of the biggest IPOs ever — at the exact moment when OpenAI's growth has slowed relative to rivals.

Executives from OpenAI have spoken with stock exchange officials about potential IPO plans, according to people familiar with the matter, but haven't yet kicked off a formal process.

IPOs are always unpredictable, but even more so when the company is in a race to develop world-changing technology, where the winner changes week to week and investor sentiment shifts just as fast.

OpenAI recently missed multiple internal revenue and user targets after facing ferocious competition from its two biggest rivals, Google and Anthropic. The losing streak has rattled executives and employees, some of whom have peppered all-hands meetings with anxious questions about the company's future.

In recent months, Friar has grown more cautious about OpenAI's data-center spending, telling company leaders she was worried the company wouldn't be able to pay for computing deals if revenue didn't grow fast enough, The Wall Street Journal has reported. She has also told them it might need more time to prepare for an IPO. Altman, on the other hand, has pushed to do it as fast as possible.

The differing approaches show just how tricky Friar's job is becoming as she helps steer OpenAI through the most crucial stretch of its eleven-year-history. If she moves too slowly, OpenAI could fall behind and hit the market behind Anthropic, missing out on pools of investor money. Move too quickly, and she could stretch the business too far.

In other words, she doesn't have much room for error.

The company said this past week that its business was "firing on all cylinders," and that Friar remained optimistic about buying more computing power. She said on LinkedIn that OpenAI's latest model was a giant leap for coding, currently the hottest application for AI.

Northern Ireland to Goldman

Friar cuts an imposing figure when she walks into any room. She is known for her strategic acumen and comfort in the corporate norms of Wall Street, including a fondness for scheduled meetings and slide decks that ensure nothing is left to chance. Altman, by contrast, likes to come up with ideas on the fly, and is known to duck out of a room almost as soon as he enters it.

Friar, 53, grew up in a mill town in Northern Ireland during the Troubles, as the three decades of conflict over the region's future are known. Her mother was a nurse and midwife, and her father worked as a personnel manager at the mill. She left for Oxford where she earned an engineering degree in metallurgy, economics and management and rowed crew.

She did an internship at a gold mine in Ghana but has said she felt she didn't feel welcome given the lack of women who were there. She went to work for McKinsey, which sent her to South Africa just after apartheid ended. She calls the period a major influence in her life.

She got her M.B.A. at Stanford, where she met her future husband. Goldman Sachs then offered her a job and helped with a visa to stay in the U.S.

Friar worked at the bank for more than a decade as an investment banker and research analyst, putting her in the running for partner. And then one morning, she got the phone call that it wasn't her turn. Friar had to hold back tears in the office, she said in a 2023 Stanford Business School interview. Her husband, David Riley, told her to see it as an opportunity to try something new.

Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff pulled her out of Goldman in 2011 to work in finance at the rising software giant. Then, just a year later, Jack Dorsey hired her as the chief financial officer of Square, a fledgling payments startup with only a few hundred employees.

One of her biggest tests came in 2015, when she had to take Square public in what was a gloomy year for IPOs. Friar struggled to drum up investor interest, who were turned off by the company's losses. Bankers told her that they would have to cut Square's share price. "It was awful, " Friar said in the Stanford interview.

On the night of pricing, they considered scrapping the IPO. But there was a sense of obligation; people had flown into New York to ring the bell. Instead, Friar and her bankers agreed to sell less than 10% of the company and price the stock at $9 a share, valuing the company at roughly $3 billion, or half its previous valuation.

Three years later, the stock had shot up.

Ready for a CEO role of her own, Friar found one in the social-networking app Nextdoor.

Nextdoor was a fraction the size of Square, both in terms of employees and valuation. The pandemic injected life into the company: people needed their neighbors for finding anything from toilet paper to friendship.

In 2021, investors poured money into newly public companies, creating the frothiest IPO market in decades. That year, the company merged with a special-purpose acquisition company, or SPAC, to list shares under the ticker KIND. Friar, who often talked about cultivating kindness in community, picked the ticker symbol.

Unlike at Square, Nextdoor's stock didn't take off. It tumbled.

The ticker may have been KIND, but the site often wasn't. Comments could be racist or just sniping between neighbors. In February 2024, Nextdoor's stock traded around $2 a share, down more than 70% from when it went public. The company announced Friar's departure.

A few months later, Friar accepted the CFO job at OpenAI.

Trying for order at OpenAI

OpenAI was squaring off against its largest shareholder Microsoft. The tech giant held significant control over the business and was fighting for a large stake in it as part of its restructuring into a for-profit corporation.

Friar worked with Goldman Sachs, where she is still held in high regard, to advise OpenAI on the negotiations. The bank is expected to play a leading role in the company's IPO.

More importantly, she had been friends with Microsoft CFO Amy Hood ever since the two worked together in their 20s at the bank. That relationship became crucial when the two companies set out to rework their technology partnership last year, in a monthslong negotiation that took a series of twists and turns.

Some OpenAI employees involved in the talks had struggled to get on the same terms as Hood, who came to be known for her tough, unsparing style, according to people familiar with the matter. Friar was a key bridge, and would sometimes fly to Microsoft's headquarters to sit down with her fellow CFO and get the deal back on track.

OpenAI and Microsoft announced a revised agreement last fall, and another one this past week.

After Meta launched a talent raid on OpenAI last year, offering its researchers pay packages that topped $100 million, Friar worked with research chief Mark Chen to come up with a new framework for approaching compensation that allowed the startup to stay financially competitive without losing discipline.

Friar has been tight-lipped about the company's public listing plans, even to some of OpenAI's closest investors. But since last fall, she has been racing to get it ready, hiring a slew of new finance and accounting professionals and kick-starting informal conversations with banks.

Then came a slowdown in OpenAI's business.

A new version of Google's Gemini app stole market share from ChatGPT, and the company missed its target of reaching 1 billion weekly active users for the chatbot by the end of last year. (It still hasn't announced this milestone.) The bleeding continued after Anthropic's coding tool Claude Code took off among software developers, and OpenAI missed some internal revenue targets.

A company spokesman said the company has internal goals that are different from what it shared with investors. He said the company hit its revenue plans in the first quarter, but declined to specify.

data.title: California Union, Billionaires Face Off Over Proposed Wealth Tax. What Comes Next? — WSJ
data.description:
By Paul Kiernan and Laura J. Nelson

California's voters are heading toward a stark choice this November: whether to levy a big tax on billionaires. They could also vote on whether to pump the brakes and make it harder to tax the state's wealthy elite.

The competition was touched off by a union push for an unprecedented tax on billionaires' assets, which proponents said has enough signatures to make the statewide ballot in the fall. Then, billionaires including the Google co-founder Sergey Brin funded two countermeasures that could undercut the proposed tax. Backers of those measures said they, too, have collected enough signatures.

Here is where things stand and what comes next.

How would the billionaire tax work?

The 2026 Billionaire Tax Act is novel in the U.S.

Rather than targeting income from work or realized capital gains, it would levy a one-time, 5% tax on the net worth of California residents with net assets of $1 billion or more. It would apply to anyone who resided in California as of Jan. 1, 2026. Valuations would be tallied Dec. 31, 2026.

Assets subject to the tax would include publicly traded stocks, bonds, private business interests, intellectual property and art. Directly owned real estate would be exempt because it is already subject to property taxes.

Billionaires would have the option to pay the bill in a single lump sum or spread it across five annual installments of 1%.

Most of the revenue would be earmarked for healthcare programs. The Service Employees International Union-United Healthcare Workers West, which proposed the tax, said it is necessary to offset a looming Medicaid funding hole owing to federal cuts.

What are the next steps?

The union said it filed more than 1.55 million signatures from California voters to county election officials. Organizers said that is a comfortable margin above the 875,000 valid signatures required to make November's ballot.

Election officials must now verify the signatures and certify the results with the California secretary of state, which has until June 25 to make a final determination on whether the measure qualifies. That is also the deadline for proponents to pull a ballot measure.

Once on the ballot, an initiative would need a simple majority vote to pass — but with a caveat because of the opposing campaigns.

What would those competing measures do?

One of the Brin-backed measures would bar new taxes on personal property, including retirement accounts, intellectual property and financial assets, excluding real estate. It would also bar retroactive taxes on those assets.

The other one would require more audits of new taxes and contains a provision about how tax revenue can be spent that directly conflicts with the wealth tax.

That measure sets up a direct clash with the campaign in favor of the billionaire tax. Under California rules, the one with the most "yes" votes takes effect if both win majority support.

The two billionaire-backed initiatives drew about 1.4 million and 1.5 million signatures, respectively, backers have said. Representatives for both campaigns said the measures are designed to apply to other ballot initiatives and legislative efforts, too.

A political organization called Building a Better California, which is funding the campaigns, has raised more than $100 million from Brin and other wealthy Californians. The wealth-tax campaign has received some $25.7 million, nearly all of it from the healthcare union.

How much money would the billionaire tax raise?

This is hotly debated. Proponents said it would generate around $100 billion in revenue over a five-year period. Opponents have said the windfall could be gradually offset by lost tax revenue as billionaires depart.

The novelty of the proposed billionaire tax makes its fiscal impact difficult to model. California's nonpartisan Legislative Analyst's Office has said the wealth tax would probably generate "tens of billions" in one-time revenue while costing "hundreds of millions of dollars or more" annually if some billionaires leave.

A number of California's ultrarich have made headlines in recent months by moving business interests outside the state or purchasing property elsewhere. They include Brin, his fellow Google co-founder, Larry Page, and Meta Platforms Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg. California has complicated requirements, though, for proving that one has truly moved beyond the reach of state tax collectors.

How many billionaires does California have?

California has a progressive tax system under which the top 1% of taxpayers paid 36.6% of the state's income taxes in 2023, the most recent year of data, according to the California Franchise Tax Board.

California has the most billionaires of any state, owing partly to Silicon Valley's tech fortunes. The wealth-intelligence firm Altrata estimated in its most recent calculation that there were 255 billionaires in California in 2024. The estimate was based on business addresses, so not all of these people would necessarily count as residents.

What do voters think?

A University of California, Berkeley, poll published in mid-March found that 52% of voters were inclined to vote "yes" on the billionaire tax, with 33% against it.

Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom, a possible 2028 presidential contender, is against the tax, warning it could weaken California's tax base and drive off investment.

A CBS News/YouGov poll conducted in late April found that 61% of voters thought California's next governor should favor "additional state taxes on Californians with assets over one billion dollars."

This explanatory article may be periodically updated.

Write to Paul Kiernan at paul.kiernan@wsj.com and Laura J. Nelson at laura.nelson@wsj.com

data.title: A Convertible That Inspires Envy Like a Luxury Handbag — WSJ
data.description:
By Dan Neil

In 1888, in an effort to stem counterfeiting of their travel trunks, Louis Vuitton and his son, Georges, introduced the Damier "checkerboard" pattern, in distinctive shades of taupe and chocolate. Fashionable folks today know it as Damier Ebene. The more intricate "LV" monogram pattern, with quatrefoil and flowers, appeared in 1896 as Georges's tribute to his late father.

But the knockoffs continued. In fact, Louis Vuitton's wallpaper-like motif became one of the most imitated designs in luxury goods, copied over the decades by haute couturiers such as Balmain, Goyard and Yves Saint Laurent, and the not-so-haute, including Coach, Burberry and Fendi. As for Louis Vuitton itself, the flagship brand of luxury conglomerate LVMH now derives some two-thirds of its revenue from sales of monogrammed accessories, particularly handbags.

Which brings us to the 2026 Mercedes-Maybach SL 680 Monogram Series ($238,950, as tested) and its eponymous monogram: a pair of Ms in a font evoking the title card of Fritz Lang's "Metropolis," enclosed in a soft-shouldered delta figure.

Originally the symbol for Maybach-Motorenbau — a maker of sternly grand luxury cars in Germany before World War II — the monogram now denotes Mercedes-Maybach, the sub-brand launched by Stuttgart in 2014 to compete against Rolls-Royce and Bentley. You may also regard the double Ms as standing for "more money."

The Monogram Series SL comes in one of two exclusive colors. Our test car dazzled in the White Ambience scheme, the color of arctic foxes, papal vestments and pharmaceutical cocaine. The alternative, Red Ambience, drenches the car in a fathomless shade of garnet metallic. In either scheme, the monogram pattern (in graphite gray) is woven into the canvas of the convertible top and printed onto the obsidian-black hood panel.

Join me now in a long, considering pause. Hmm. Set aside for a moment whether this two-seat, 577-hp luxury convertible, this bazooka with a mink silencer, is any fun to drive. Is it in strictly good taste? Does it say things that owners would like to be said? Or is it, at long last, too much, too obvious? With the Monogram Series, has Mercedes-Maybach finally slipped into "Crazy Rich Asians" territory?

(Speaking of over the top: When I interviewed Carlos Ghosn at the 2010 Paris Motor Show, the then-CEO of Nissan was wearing a tailored suit from Louis Vuitton gridded with the LV monogram pattern. I dared tease him about it; I said it looked like he had his suit on inside out. Our time together was brief.)

Whatever my misgivings about the 680 SL's seemliness, I soon met a lady who convinced me I was wrong. I say met — we were both turning right at a four-way stop, I in the 680 SL and she in a white-over-black GMC Tahoe. The look she gave the car as we passed was priceless — a look of sudden, obscene envy, mingled with a kind of fury. This nice lady was this close to committing a carjacking. I guess the monogram thing works.

The name Maybach has a father-and-son history, too. Wilhelm, the father, was one of the pioneers of German automotive engineering; he and Karl founded what became the Maybach company in 1909, manufacturing high-performance engines for zeppelins, airplanes and luxury cars until the start of World War II. In 1960, Daimler-Benz bought what was left of the Maybach company. In 1997, the company launched Maybach as an independent division, offering two stupendous limousines, the 57 (as in 5.7 meters long) and the 62, from 2002 to 2013.

The reborn Maybach struggled for sales against the old-world incumbents, Rolls-Royce and Bentley. In the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, Maybach was reorganized as Mercedes-Maybach — a sub-brand building small numbers of technically evolved, superluxury variants of mainline products.

There is little chance of mistaking the test car for typical Mercedes fare. Up front, a fierce rictus of sterling-bright, illuminated vertical bars is capped with a chrome plate embossed with the word "Maybach." The traditional three-pointed-star hood ornament is reduced to a mere outcropping in a sea of monograms. While not as obvious, the lower grille area is also dotted with monograms. I'm beginning to see a pattern.

The construction is immaculate. Few automobile exteriors are ever finished like this, with its broad, sterling-like chrome garnishes at the lower grille intakes, the rocker panels, exhaust outlets and bumpers, all smoothed perfectly into the blown-glass curvatures. The only exterior detail I couldn't come to terms with were the rose-gold accents in the headlamp assemblies. Not ever.

Inside, the cabin of this not particularly big roadster is upholstered like a ring box from Van Cleef & Arpels, wrapped in "Crystal White" Nappa leather, including the rear parcel bench, with matching seats embossed with floral designs and Mercedes-Maybach emblems. The deep-pile floor mats are also white — or at least were, before I got to them.

What isn't white is black leather (the dash top), polished black wood, with silver chrome trim. At night — with the top down, the Burmester audio bumping, the snowhills of white leather upholstery glowing with LED indirect lighting — the space feels a bit like sitting neck deep at a Miami Beach hot-tub party. Dan's up in the house. Wut up, ladies who lunch?

Is there actually a car in there somewhere? Oh yeah. They could have called it the Drano Edition because they threw in the kitchen sink. The list of wonders begins with the Quiet Storm: the handbuilt 4.0-liter twin-turbo V8, summoning 577 ghostly horses with soft-soled trainers on their hooves, galloping across the countryside and destroying driving records.

I got a speeding ticket in South Carolina. No excuses. But, man, I had completely lost track of my speed. This car's propulsion system — including a nine-speed automatic transmission and fully variable all-wheel drive — is so heavily damped and isolated it might as well be in another car. Sport mode will awaken a growl in the exhaust pipes but that's not what pays the SL 680's bills.

Other overachieving systems — including the active hydraulic roll stabilization, the rear-wheel steering, even the acoustically insulated convertible top, your honor — contributed to my oblivious delinquency.

Just another fashion victim.

2026 Mercedes-Maybach SL 680 Monogram Series

Base price: $224,900

Price, as tested: $238,950

Powertrain: twin-turbocharged 4.0-liter DOHC V8; nine-speed automatic transmission; fully variable full-time AWD.

Power/torque: 577hp at 5,500-6,500 rpm/590 lb-ft at 2,500-5,000 rpm

Curb weight: 4,409 pounds

0-60 mph: 4.0 seconds

Length/wheelbase/width/height: 184.9/106.3/82.7/53.5 inches

Top speed 161 mph: (electronically limited)

EPA fuel economy: 13/20/16 mpg city/highway/combined)

Cargo capacity: 7.5/8.5 cubic feet (top down/up)

data.title: Coinbase says deal reached on key provision of crypto bill
data.description:
Coinbase said on Friday a deal has been reached on a key provision in a landmark crypto legislation that could clear the path for the bill to move forward in the U.S. Senate.

  • The bill stalled earlier this year because banks opposed a provision allowing stablecoin issuers and crypto firms to offer yield-bearing products and other rewards paid on stablecoins that could lure away bank deposits, making it harder for them to fund lending.

  • Crypto giants such as Coinbase said they must be able to offer rewards to recruit customers and that barring them would be anticompetitive.

  • "In the end, the banks were able to get more restrictions on rewards, but we protected what matters – the ability for Americans to earn rewards, based on real usage of crypto platforms and networks," Coinbase's Chief Policy Officer Faryar Shirzad said in a post on X.

  • Punchbowl News, which reported the text of the compromise finalized by Senators Thom Tillis and Angela Alsobrooks, said the language includes a broad prohibition on rewards offered "in a manner that is economically or functionally equivalent to the payment of interest or yield on an interest-bearing bank deposit".

  • The text also directs regulators to propose a new series of stablecoin regulations, including the development of a new stablecoin disclosure regime and a list of permissible reward activities, Punchbowl News added. Reuters could not immediately verify the report.

  • Crypto companies have been operating in a regulatory gray area, which executives say has stymied their businesses. The proposed Clarity Act aims to create clear regulations that should help promote cryptocurrency adoption.

  • President Donald Trump, who courted crypto cash on the campaign trail and whose family has profited from its own token, has prioritized crypto reform during his second administration.

data.title: TNXP Slips After Hours On Slower Than Expected Launch For Fibromyalgia Drug
data.description:
Key points:
  • Overall net revenue from sales in Q1 was $8.3 million, the company said. 
  • The company said that it now has funds to sustain itself till the second quarter of 2027.
  • Total net revenue from sales of Tonmya since launch through the end of the first quarter was $5.1 million.

Shares of Tonix Pharmaceuticals (TNXP) fell 3% after hours after the company said that it expects a wider preliminary net loss for the first quarter of 2026 and revenue of $3.7 million for its newly launched drug Tonmya.

The company said on Friday that it expects a Q1 net loss of about $40.8 million compared to a loss of $16.8 million for the corresponding quarter of 2025.

TNXP’s Preliminary Numbers

Q1 net revenue from sales of its treatments Zembrace SymTouch, and Tosymra for the quarter ended March 31, 2026, was approximately $3.2 million, compared to $2.4 million for the quarter ended March 31, 2025.

Net revenue from sales of Tonmya for the quarter was approximately $3.7 million. Tonmya, an FDA-approved, non-opioid medication designed to treat fibromyalgia in adults, was launched in November 2025.

Total net revenue from sales of Tonmya since launch through the end of the first quarter was $5.1 million, Tonix said.

The company said that it ended the first quarter with about $185.5 million in cash and cash equivalents, which, together with net proceeds of $17.4 million that it raised in April 2026 to be enough to sustain it till the second quarter of 2027.

The company is now slated to report its full financial results on May 11.

How Did TNXP Retail Traders React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around TNXP stock stayed in the ‘bearish’ territory over the past 24 hours, while message volume stayed at ‘normal’ levels.

A Stocktwits user opined that the numbers put forth by Tonix are being “misunderstood” and Tonmya launch is not “as weak as it appears.”

https://www.stocktwits.com/ltinvestorx/message/652073834

Another user opined that the company entered 2026 with real momentum and a stronger balance sheet than many of its small-cap biotech peers.

https://www.stocktwits.com/ACMTrader/message/652072480

TNXP stock fell 25% over the past 12 months.

data.title: Sorry, Texas. There's Still a Ton of Money in Wall Street North. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andy Serwer

You hear a lot these days about burgeoning Sunbelt money centers, in particular Dallas (called Y'all Street), where new trading outposts like the Texas Stock Exchange are kicking up their heels.

What about Wall Street North? Though Bostonians would be loath to call it that, trillions of invested assets slosh around Beantown (a moniker locals consider cringy) and a lot more legacy than in Texas. While these are just Fenway Park numbers, Boston has some $15 trillion of AUM, and Texas looks to have about the same, mostly under management at Charles Schwab.

The bread and butter of Boston asset management has been actively managed equity mutual funds, a business that has been crimped by investors choosing exchange-traded funds, index funds, and alternative investments instead. Are Boston firms facing this challenge?

In fact, they are.

Considering all this — and as I was in town for a local footrace — I decided to check in on Boston's huge, below-the-radar money world. Like much of Boston, it goes back to the Brahmins, the elite caste of the city, among whom, according to the doggerel, "The Lowells talk only to Cabots, and the Cabots talk only to God." (For more on this topic, a good read is Puritan Boston and Quaker Philadelphia.)

The origin of Boston money management goes back to law firms like Choate Hall & Stewart and Hemenway & Barnes, which uniquely have investment management businesses tucked into their practices.

"There were large fortunes made in the China trade and then in textiles, " says Newcomb Stillwell, a retired co-managing partner in the Boston office of Ropes & Gray, which recently spun off its money management business. "Around the middle of the 19th century, entrepreneurs who founded these companies started worrying about the things all first-generation rich people worry about: Can my kids handle running these companies? Lawyers began acting as trustees, and that evolved into money management." (For more, check out The Boston Trustee and Brahmin Capitalism.)

Edward "Ted" Crosby Johnson, who founded Fidelity Management & Research in 1946, and grandfather of FMR's current CEO, Abby Johnson, worked as a lawyer at Ropes & Gray. When he left the firm, Johnson associated with the founders of Boston's MFS Investment Management, which created the first mutual fund in 1924, and George Putnam, founder of Putnam Investments. Other Boston contemporaries included Paul Cabot, Richard Saltonstall, and Richard Paine, founders of State Street Investment. (Not to be confused with State Street Corp., which has $5.7 trillion under management.)

Today Fidelity, still family-owned and with $7 trillion in assets, looms large over the city's — and the nation's — money-management business, with $37.7 billion in revenue and $12.7 billion in net income last year. BlackRock has $14 trillion in assets under management but only $24.2 billion in revenue and $5.5 billion in profit.

Fidelity executives told me that one in five Americans have a relationship with the firm and that it continues to increase market share. In the Department of Change category, Fido, as some call it, highlighted its stablecoin and two new ETFs in its recent first-quarter outlook. (A book on the firm, House of Fidelity, was also just published.)

Blocks away from Fidelity in the Financial District are the storied Boston firms of Eaton Vance and Loomis Sayles. And around the corner, by the Fort Point Channel and near the Boston Tea Party Ships & Museum, sits rival Wellington. (None of the firms has much in the way of signage.)

Wellington, with $1.3 trillion in AUM, manages money for institutions such as Vanguard's Wellington Fund (co-run by Daniel Pozen, nephew of longstanding Fidelity executive Bob Pozen). Wellington's back story is another Boston tangled tale. Founded in Philadelphia and chaired by Jack Bogle, who later founded Vanguard, Wellington merged with Boston's Thorndike, Doran, Paine & Lewis in 1967. The latter group took control of the firm in the 1970s with Nick Thorndike as chairman.

Thorndike, who cut his teeth at Fidelity, has two sons in the business: Lanny is the president of Choate Hall's investment advisory, and Will, founder of private-equity firm Housatonic Partners, and the author of the Warren Buffett-recommended investment book The Outsiders.

"We've been focused on growing our alternatives business," says Wellington's CEO Jean Hynes, who joined the firm in 1991, fresh out of Wellesley College. "It's $50 billion and will become more important as we've expanded talent there recently." Such talent may be in demand.

"Boston has created a very strong position in the mutual fund industry," an old-line Bostonian tells me. "Now there's a whole constellation of private-equity firms and a decent hedge fund industry. Lots of new fortunes have been made."

Some new fortunes were birthed out of Harvard Management, which runs Harvard's $56.9 billion endowment — overseen by uncle and nephew Paul and Walter Cabot in the latter half of the last century. Walter diversified the portfolio into alternatives, which was expanded by his successor, legendary fund manager Jack Meyer, who attracted a number of talented money managers. Some would go on to form their own firms, like Jon Jacobson at Highfields Capital Management, one of Boston's most successful hedge funds.

"Boston's changed a lot over the past 35 years," says Jacobson. "Abby Johnson has done an incredible job. Jean Hynes too. And Nancy Zimmerman runs a fixed-income firm called Bracebridge, which has done quite well."

How about that? Three of the most prominent money men of Boston are now women.

Ben Williams, portfolio manager at wealth-management firm Welch & Forbes, founded in 1838, puts a fine point on it. "We're always adapting to change," he says. "But we have great tradition here too."

Take that, Texas.

Corrections & Amplifications: Assets under management in Texas total about $15 trillion. An earlier version of this article incorrectly put the figure at around $4 trillion.

Write to Andy Serwer at andy.serwer@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Netflix, in a First, Plans Full Theatrical Release for 'Narnia' — WSJ
data.description:
By Ben Fritz

In a move theater operators and filmmakers have been pushing for years, Netflix said Friday it will for the first time release a big-budget movie exclusively in cinemas worldwide for seven weeks.

"Narnia: The Magician's Nephew," from "Barbie" director Greta Gerwig, will debut in theaters Feb. 12 of next year and then on Netflix on April 2, the company said.

Netflix has previously dabbled with theatrical releases, typically putting movies in several hundred cinemas for a few weeks at most before premiering them online. It has also experimented with special events, like a "KPop Demon Hunters" big-screen sing-along.

It has never given a movie the type of release traditional Hollywood studios do, in thousands of theaters around the world for more than a month before it is available to watch at home.

Co-Chief Executive Ted Sarandos has previously called such releases "an outmoded idea for most people" and said Netflix makes movies for people to watch on Netflix.

Adam Aron, chief executive of AMC Theatres, called it a "massive development" and said his chain, the biggest in the U.S., will aggressively support the "Narnia" release.

"It should not be lost on anyone the significance of Netflix trying a real global release of a major film title," he wrote on X.

Sarandos began to change his tune on the value of theatrical releases when his company agreed to pay $72 billion to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery, which owns one of Hollywood's biggest theatrical distribution operations, late last year. After losing that deal to Paramount, Sarandos met with theater operators at the CinemaCon industry convention last month, though he didn't disclose the "Narnia" plan there, according to people familiar with the matter.

Netflix previously planned to release "Narnia" exclusively in IMAX late this November, 30 days before it was to start streaming on Christmas. Some theater owners said they weren't sure they would play the film because they have been demanding that studios give movies an exclusive theatrical run of at least 45 days to incentivize people to go to the cinema, rather than wait to watch a film at home.

IMAX has nearly 1,800 locations worldwide, less than 1% of all movie screens worldwide, meaning "Narnia" would have had a very limited theatrical footprint. Gerwig, one of Hollywood's most in-demand directors, has been advocating for the widest possible theatrical release for "Narnia," people familiar with the matter said. The movie will now debut on IMAX screens on Feb. 10, two days before its full release.

Netflix, which releases dozens of films online every year, isn't currently planning to give any other motion pictures a robust worldwide theatrical release like "Narnia." But if Gerwig's movie is successful, more could follow, a person familiar with the matter said.

That would be a boon for theaters, which are struggling with a roughly one-third drop in attendance since before the pandemic and have been pleading with Hollywood for more major releases to draw audiences. The "Narnia" move could help Netflix get into business with more A-list filmmakers who only want to work with studios that will give their movies full theatrical releases.

Write to Ben Fritz at ben.fritz@wsj.com
